primer.markdown:
# Publish the server; submit the install command

You have built a server, wired it into Claude Code, and watched it answer a `tools/call`. The last step is the one that matters to anyone but you: turning your local binary into something a stranger can install with one command.

The shape to imitate is the meta-prompting-plugin's own `README.md`. Look at its roadmap: `v0.1.0` is a git-clone install, `v0.2.0` promises a TypeScript library and an MCP server, and `v0.3.0` aims at the Claude Code marketplace. That is the arc every server author walks. You start as `git clone && ./INSTALL.sh`, you harden into a versioned package on npm or PyPI, and — when the marketplace accepts your submission — you reduce to a one-liner install: `claude mcp add <your-server>`.

The sovereignty clause of this Quest is the same one Forge BARQUE ends on. You do not finish holding a certificate; you finish holding a URL and an install command that teaches the next learner what you built. **Submit that install command as your Quest proof.** In Phase 1 of LUXOR Academy the submission is captured locally and queued for the codebase-to-course pipeline. In Phase 3 the pipeline runs live: it extracts your README, your capability declaration, and one representative tool handler, validates them against `validators/slots.schema.json`, and mints a permalink Quest that teaches *your* MCP server by the same six-slot contract you just completed.

You came in knowing what MCP was. You leave with a server other people can install.
primer.key_insight: The canonical last module is always a ship step. Your install command is the Quest proof — it turns learner into author, and your repo into the next learner's curriculum.

visual.alt: A three-station release ladder. Bottom rung: a `git clone && ./INSTALL.sh` terminal icon labelled `v0.1.0 — early adopters`. Middle rung: an npm package icon labelled `v0.2.0 — versioned release`. Top rung: a Claude Code marketplace ribbon labelled `v0.3.0 — claude mcp add <your-server>`. An arrow labelled `Quest proof` points from a learner-submitted repo card upward through the ladder, ending at a permalink ribbon that reads `luxor.academy/quest/<your-server>`.

sandbox_policy.csp: default-src 'none'; style-src 'unsafe-inline'; script-src 'unsafe-inline'; img-src data:; font-src https://fonts.gstatic.com; connect-src 'none'; frame-ancestors 'self'

interactive.success_criterion: Learner pastes a GitHub repo URL and a proposed install command (e.g. `claude mcp add <name> -- npx -y @scope/my-mcp`) into the form. The simulator previews what the Phase 3 codebase-to-course pipeline will extract — README title, capability declaration, a representative tool — and records the submission in `localStorage` under `luxor.academy.completed.mcp-from-zero.06`. The submit button is a visual stub in Phase 1; live pipeline activation lands in Phase 3.

artifact.excerpt:
## Status & roadmap

- **v0.1.0** — plugin-only: 5 commands, 17 skills, curated theory docs. You are here.
- **v0.2.0** — TypeScript library (`@meta-prompting/core`, fp-ts based) + MCP server.
- **v0.3.0** — marketplace submission, richer quality visualizations, property-based skill tests in CI.

questions.prompt: Per `README.md` §Status & roadmap, in which release does the meta-prompting-plugin ship an MCP server alongside its TypeScript library?
questions.explanation: README.md line 343: `**v0.2.0** — TypeScript library (@meta-prompting/core, fp-ts based) + MCP server.` The shipping arc of any MCP project starts as a git-clone plugin, hardens into a versioned library paired with a server, and ends at marketplace submission.

questions.prompt: What distinguishes a `v0.3.0`-style marketplace install from the `v0.1.0` git-clone install?
questions.explanation: A marketplace install collapses to a single `claude mcp add <name>` line that the client resolves from its registry. Git-clone installs require the user to pick a checkout path, run the installer, and wire the launch line by hand — three steps where the marketplace needs one.

questions.prompt: Why is the canonical last module of every LUXOR Academy Quest a 'Ship Your Own' step rather than a summary quiz?
questions.explanation: Sovereignty. The Academy's canonical-last-module pattern requires learners to leave each Quest holding a permalink to their own work — an install command, a repo URL, a published server — not a score. A summary quiz trains for the Quest. Ship-Your-Own trains for the next repo.
